description: A room, building, or clearly defined work area becomes a 'keritha' when it is arranged for making and repair. Tools and benches may share the space with stock and unfinished work, with enough room to use them safely. 'phaewi' (studio) is arranged for artistic work; a workshop is arranged for practical craft.
articulatory_notes: Close the back of the tongue against the soft palate for unvoiced 'k' and release into middle-height front 'e'. Tap or briefly trill the tongue tip at the alveolar ridge for 'r', opening into stressed high-front unrounded 'i'. Rest the tongue tip lightly against the upper front teeth for unvoiced 'th' and finish with central 'a'.
sound_symbolism: A back closure begins the word, a quick tongue tap turns its middle, and the final syllable releases through dental 'th' into 'a'. The three contacts feel distinct and orderly, like tools returned to familiar places.
usage_notes: As a place noun, 'keritha' takes ordinary locative prepositions. The noun itself leaves ownership, access, supervision, and the people working there unspecified. A temporary outdoor work area can also be a 'keritha' if it has been deliberately arranged for the task.

pillars.solarpunk-values: A shared workshop lets tools remain in common use and makes repair available without requiring every household to own the same equipment.
pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: Working side by side lets people notice sound, pressure, posture, and sequence while useful work continues.

semantic_domains.activity: a place arranged to support practical work
semantic_domains.creation: space and equipment for making or repair
semantic_domains.spatial: a room, building, or bounded work area
